moveAnnotations.annotation: The central pawn advance claims space and opens lines for the queen and light-squared bishop, establishing White's typical first-move advantage.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e4 is the Keres Variation move, claiming the center and opening lines for queen and bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds with the Sicilian Defense, the most popular and combative response to 1.e4. Instead of matching us in the center with 1...e5, our opponent immediately challenges our central control from the side, preparing to fianchetto the queen's bishop or advance ...d6 and ...Nf6. The c5 pawn controls the important d4 square, making it harder for us to establish the ideal pawn center with d2-d4. This asymmetrical pawn structure typically leads to sharp, unbalanced positions where both sides attack on opposite flanks.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Sicilian c5 controls d4, preventing the ideal pawn center and leading to sharp opposite-flank attacks.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the rare Keres Variation, developing our knight to an unusual square that keeps maximum flexibility in our pawn structure. Unlike the standard 2.Nf3, this knight placement doesn't block the f-pawn, allowing us to potentially play f2-f4 later to support a kingside attack. The knight from e2 can jump to several useful squares: g3 to support kingside play, d4 to occupy the center, or c3 to support d4 if we choose to advance it. While this system is less forcing than the sharp main lines of the Sicilian, it offers solid development and the chance to transpose into various setups depending on how Black responds, making it a favorite of players who prefer strategic complexity over memorizing extensive theory.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to e2 keeps the f-pawn flexible for f2-f4, supporting a kingside attack while allowing jumps to g3, d4, or c3.
